text: The use of immune correlates of protection (CoP) to infer vaccine efficacy is a scientifically validated and regulatory-endorsed strategy in vaccine approvals. A well-established precedent comes from influenza vaccine development, where a hemagglutination inhibition (HI) titer of 1:40 has historically been accepted as conferring approximately 50% protection against symptomatic infection [ 1 , 2 ]. This threshold has informed the evaluation and approval of influenza vaccines. Once a CoP is agreed upon by regulatory authorities, it enables the demonstration of efficacy for new vaccines through traditional non-inferiority criteria. This approach is referred to as immunobridging and it involves comparing either the proportion of vaccine recipients who achieve the protective threshold or the geometric mean titers (GMT) relative to those elicited by a licensed comparator vaccine. The application of immunobridging entails ethical and regulatory considerations around evidentiary standards.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: More recently, similar principles have been applied to COVID-19. Emerging data demonstrate a robust quantitative relationship between neutralizing antibody levels and observed clinical efficacy across multiple vaccine platforms [ 3 , 4 ]. These findings support the use of immunological markers such as neutralizing titers as reliable surrogate endpoints for vaccine protection. In the setting of rapidly evolving SARS-CoV-2 variants and the global shift away from placebo-controlled trials, the immunobridging approach offers a scientifically valid and ethically appropriate pathway for vaccine assessment and regulatory decision-making.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: CoPs are not limited to active immunization such as vaccines; they are also relevant to passive immunization strategies, including monoclonal antibodies (mAbs). Serum virus neutralizing antibody (sVNA) titers and serum drug concentrations serve as reliable surrogate endpoints to support efficacy across mAb platforms. In the absence of placebo-controlled trials, especially in immunocompromised (IC) populations or against ever-emergent variants, these markers enable variant-specific or population-based immunobridging, aligned with regulatory precedent and model-informed drug development strategies. The relationship between sVNA titers derived from mAbs and protection against COVID-19 has been explored using clinical study data in immune-naïve individuals prior to widespread vaccination and immune exposure [ 4 , 5 ].

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Pemivibart was the first (and to date, only) SARS-CoV-2 mAb to effectively use the immunobridging approach to obtain Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) for prevention of COVID-19 in certain immunocompromised individuals [ 6 , 7 ]. Data from a single-arm cohort (cohort A) of the two-cohort CANOPY phase 3 trial ( NCT06039449 ) including 306 IC individuals were used to establish immunobridging. Cohort B of the study randomized 482 non-IC individuals in a 2:1 ratio to receive either pemivibart or placebo. In addition to assessing safety, the trial evaluated the efficacy of pemivibart in preventing symptomatic COVID-19. Both cohorts were conducted concurrently, and all participants were followed for 12 months.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Participants received 4500 mg of pemivibart intravenously on day 1 and month 3. The analysis of the primary endpoint in cohort A compared sVNA titers calculated for pemivibart (pharmacokinetic [PK] concentration/IC 50 of select relevant variant) with those calculated for the prototype parent mAb adintrevimab against the Delta variant predominant during the EVADE trial.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: In the EVADE study ( NCT04859517 ), adintrevimab 300 mg provided 71% protection from symptomatic COVID-19 through 3 months [ 8 ]. EVADE was prematurely suspended due to the circulating variant shift from Delta to Omicron lineages, in which adintrevimab demonstrated a decline in neutralizing activity. The titer value of adintrevimab against Delta at month 3 (1:3514) was used as the immunobridging target, as substantial data from later time points were unavailable.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Immunobridging in the CANOPY IC cohort A was achieved if the lower limit of the 90% confidence interval of the geometric mean ratio (GMR) between the selected titer target and the mAb/variant-specific sVNA titer was > 0.80. Strict immunobridging of pemivibart against the JN.1 variant (dominant at the time of analysis) to adintrevimab through 3 months was not met (GMR 0.70 [90% CI 0.68–0.72]); however, a complementary immunobridging method using data from a meta-analysis showed that the titer values for pemivibart were consistent with the ranges observed in previous clinical trials of other mAbs with proven clinical efficacy [ 4 – 6 , 9 ]. Authorization was granted based on the totality of evidence [ 6 ].

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Although strict immunobridging predicting pemivibart efficacy at 3 months was not met, pemivibart exhibited a 94% relative risk reduction (RRR) in prevention of RT-PCR-confirmed COVID-19 at 3 months and a 74% RRR over 12 months against a range of dominant circulating variants compared with placebo in the CANOPY non-IC cohort B (Table 1 ). The efficacy results indicate that the immunobridging target was set too high to accurately predict clinically meaningful efficacy. Notably, the clinical efficacy of pemivibart through month 12 was associated with neutralizing titer levels as low as approximately 1:50 against KP.3.1.1 (Table 1 ), the dominant circulating variant at that time in the study, which is substantially lower than the previously considered target of 1:3514 for meaningful efficacy [ 6 ]. These data demonstrate the importance of collecting correlative data at later time points when serum concentrations have waned to fully characterize the relationship between sVNA titers and clinical efficacy, which was not possible in the EVADE study due to limited follow-up.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Data from other studies have likewise supported efficacy with lower neutralizing titers. In an exploratory analysis of the post-Omicron portion of EVADE, adintrevimab provided a 60% RRR against Omicron BA.1 at 1 month; the associated calculated titer was 1:33 [ 4 ]. This target is also in the range of what was seen with tixagevimab/cilgavimab 300 mg/300 mg against Omicron BA.1, where titers of 1:71 at 6 months were expected to provide slightly greater than 70% protection against symptomatic disease [ 10 , 11 ].

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Cumulative data with SARS-CoV-2-directed mAbs indicate that calculated titers can serve as surrogate immunobridging endpoints that are highly likely to predict efficacy; however, the immunobridging target must be refined to more precisely reflect the titers that likely result in clinical protection based on neutralizing titers and clinical efficacy analysis over a continuum of time points.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Most clinical efficacy data to date comes from studies performed in non-IC populations. The only randomized phase 3 trial that evaluated the efficacy of a mAb in IC individuals was the SUPERNOVA trial that assessed sipavibart 300 mg for the prevention of symptomatic COVID-19 (Haidar 2025). The SUPERNOVA trial demonstrated a RRR of 42.9% ( p = 0.0012) of sipavibart 300 mg in preventing symptomatic COVID-19 in IC participants by susceptible Omicron XBB- and JN.1-related variants [ 12 ]. Efficacy was modest compared to mAb studies in non-IC participants (CANOPY, EVADE, or PROVENT), where RRRs of > 70% were observed. These data may suggest that the overall level of efficacy achieved in IC individuals may be lower at similar neutralizing titer levels.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: The methodology described in this paper proposes a predictive tool for efficacy using neutralizing titers to inform clinical development for related mAbs developed on the same platform for prevention of COVID-19 in both IC and non-IC populations.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Data from both cohorts of the CANOPY trial were used to develop a Cox proportional hazards model with time-varying covariate as a statistical immune CoP model. The model was selected as it allows for antibody titer decay over time, incorporates variant-specific IC 50 updates for emergent variants, accommodates varying follow-up times and right-censoring among participants who remained uninfected, and estimates the instantaneous hazard of infection as a function of contemporaneous antibody levels. The time-varying covariate was the calculated sVNA, which changes over time in response to the emergence and prevalence of different SARS-CoV-2 variants. sVNA was calculated at 2-week intervals by integrating drug concentration data with weighted IC 50 values of the circulating variants as detailed below.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Two separate regression models were constructed for IC and non-IC participants using clinical data up to 12 months and estimated pemivibart concentrations from cohort A (IC) and cohort B (non-IC) participants, respectively. The models used time from the first dosing to the first RT-PCR-confirmed symptomatic COVID-19 as the dependent variable and daily weighted titer levels as the independent variable. The predictor (independent) variable was calculated sVNA levels estimated from the drug concentrations divided by pseudovirus neutralization assay IC 50 against a relevant variant.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: As cohort A did not have a placebo control, to enable a comparable estimation of clinical efficacy, placebo data from the contemporaneously randomized cohort B were used as the control group in the model. Additional details on the model development and key diagnostics (Figs. S1 , S2 ) are available in the supplementary material.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Clinical studies span periods with multiple circulating variants. Figure 1 presents the SARS-CoV-2 variants during the CANOPY study time frame. Five predominant SARS-CoV-2 variants circulated during the study along with numerous other variants with notably different IC 50 values. A neutralizing titer variable was identified and included in the Cox model for calculating neutralizing titer to account for the complexity introduced by variant variability. We developed the weighted average IC 50 concentration for a given time frame by summing the product of each variant’s proportion ( w ) and its closest representative pseudovirus IC 50 value over the time frame:

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Variant proportions were sourced from the CDC variant tracker; pemivibart’s pseudovirus IC 50 values were determined as previously described [ 7 ]. When the IC 50 was unavailable for a variant, it was assigned the closest match based on phylogenetic proximity using PANGOLIN lineage analysis. Variants with minor fractions classified as “other” by the CDC were excluded. Given pemivibart’s robust IC 50 dataset covering major variants, this method provides a reasonable approximation. The present model assumes the calculated sVNA titers for a collection of circulating variants, after being weighted by their prevalence, informs the infection risk. If a resistant variant becomes more dominant in prevalence, the model would take into account the reduced efficacy and/or lower calculated sVNA titers.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Daily weighted neutralizing titer was obtained by dividing average pemivibart serum concentration of each day by the weighted IC 50 . The average pemivibart concentration of each day from the first dose was estimated using post hoc PK parameters of a population PK model developed for CANOPY participants; weighted IC 50 value was updated every 2 weeks (aligned with the frequency by which the CDC variant tracker updated to allow for the highest granularity of the data) based on calendar days through the study period. For each participant, the predicted daily average pemivibart concentration and weighted IC 50 value were matched by calendar days and the correspondent daily weighted neutralizing titer was calculated. sVNA titers were further log-transformed (log 10 ).

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: The daily risk of acquiring COVID-19 from the first dose through 365 days was estimated using a time-varying Cox proportional hazards model, the hazard function is specified as:

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Since the weighted titer is derived from the predicted daily pemivibart PK concentration, placebo participants were assigned to a log 10 calculated titer value of 0. This zero-value assignment effectively created a natural dichotomous treatment contrast. Consequently, the estimated exp( β ) can be interpreted as the hazard ratio corresponding to a tenfold increase in titer relative to the placebo baseline. The quantity 1 − HR( X ) represents the relative reduction in the hazard of symptomatic COVID-19 from any titer level X and is widely used as a measure of clinical efficacy for preventive interventions, reflecting the reduction in risk associated with pemivibart exposure. Model fitting was conducted using R version 4.3.3 [ 13 ].

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: This model is variant-agnostic, allowing it to be applied to any variant with a known IC 50 .

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: The CANOPY trial was conducted in accordance with the International Council for Harmonization guideline on Good Clinical Practice, the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki, and all applicable regulations. Written informed consent was obtained from all participants for participation in the study and publication. The protocol was approved by Advarra IRB, Reference Code Pro00070754, and has been published previously [ 6 ].

text: The fitted model using the data in CANOPY study, cohort B yielded β = − 0.4384 (standard error = 0.1009, likelihood ratio test p value < 0.0001); hence the hazard ratio as a function of titer level X (on the log 10 scale) is given by:

text: The model with cohort A data combined with cohort B placebo data yielded β = − 0.2566 (standard error = 0.0832, likelihood ratio test p value = 0.00172). The equation for predicting HR from the log 10 transformed titer level X becomes:

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Figure 2 displays the predicted curves for weighted titers for cohorts A and B, accompanied by percentile-based 95% confidence band derived from 1000 bootstrap samples. Table 2 summarizes the efficacy predictions from the Cox model for selected target titers, indicating that a titer level of 1:50 corresponds to an estimated 52% relative reduction in hazard in non-IC. In the absence of informative censoring and under the proportional hazards assumption, the clinical efficacy obtained from the Cox proportional hazards model (1 − HR) is expected to be similar to relative risk reduction estimated from a binary endpoint analysis particularly when event rates are low and follow-up is comparable across treatment groups. Table 2 also presents the number needed to treat (NNT) for 6-month protection, calculated as 1/(efficacy × 0.12) where 0.12 is the 6-month attack rate for placebo group. It is important to note that NNT is highly dependent on the attack rate among untreated participants. For any assumed attack rate, the NNT can be recalculated by replacing 0.12 in the formula with the corresponding value.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Efficacy increased with sVNA titer in a non-linear manner, with smaller incremental gains at higher concentrations. Predicted efficacy was lower at all titer levels in the IC cohort.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: In non-IC populations, maintaining target sVNA titers of 1:500 at the end of the dosing interval is associated with approximately 70% or greater relative reduction in hazard against currently dominant SARS-CoV-2 variants. This threshold provides a margin for continued protection against emerging variants with reduced susceptibility. For instance, a two-fold reduction in titer (to 1:250) results in less than a 5% decrease in predicted efficacy.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: For IC populations, higher sVNA titers may be required to achieve protection levels comparable to those observed in non-IC individuals. At the end of the dosing interval, target titers of 1:500 are associated with approximately 50% or greater relative reduction in hazard against currently circulating variants. To achieve efficacy levels closer to those in non-IC populations, increased dosing frequency or higher dose density would likely be necessary to elevate titers accordingly.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: In the phase 3 CANOPY trial, participants received 4500 mg of pemivibart intravenously on day 1 and month 3, with surrogate efficacy based on sVNA titers validating immunobridging as a reliable predictor of mAb effectiveness [ 6 , 14 ]. However, the very high titer from the EVADE study that pemivibart was required to reach for successful immunobridging forced a very high dose of pemivibart to be administered. The clinical efficacy of pemivibart in preventing RT-PCR-confirmed COVID-19 over 12 months in placebo-controlled cohort B suggested that much lower titers are associated with clinical efficacy (Table 1 ).

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: By developing a time-varying Cox proportional hazards model based on CANOPY clinical data up to 12 months and estimated pemivibart concentrations from CANOPY cohorts A and B participants, respectively, selection of dosage level and route based on modeling is possible for future Invivyd platform mAbs to optimize the benefit-risk profile.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Neutralizing titers as a surrogate marker have a clear mechanistic link to therapeutic effect, correlate with clinical outcomes, and are adaptable to the changing sublineages of COVID-19. The major merits of such an immunobridging target (Table 2 ) and associated titer-efficacy curve (Fig. 2 ) are that (1) the proposed target provides sufficient efficacy whereby real clinical benefit can still be conferred at lower titer levels that may result from drift in mAb IC 50 over time; and (2) since it is certain that the mAb IC 50 estimates will change periodically with new variants, leveraging such a continuous quantitative relationship between IC 50 and expected clinical efficacy provides an intelligible and actionable alteration to product description for healthcare providers, regulators, and sponsors to assess product risk/benefit in day-to-day clinical practice.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: The predicted efficacy curve from the CANOPY cohort B data is consistent with the findings from the logistic regression modeling introduced by Stadler et al. [ 5 ], who analyzed publicly available summary-level data from randomized controlled trials evaluating mAbs for the prevention of symptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection primarily in non-IC populations. By modeling the dose–response relationship using in vivo mAb concentrations (normalized by in vitro IC 50 value) and corresponding protection levels over time, they showed that high neutralizing titers (e.g., > 1:500, which corresponds to approximately 70% efficacy, compared to 69% efficacy in our Cox model) are consistently linked to strong prophylactic efficacy across multiple mAbs. The curve originally introduced by Stadler et al. [ 5 ] was subsequently updated by Schmidt et al. with pemivibart data [ 6 ].

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: It is important to note that, according to the Cox model, as titers increase above 1:500, the corresponding gains in efficacy diminish. For instance, doubling the titer from 1:50 to 1:100 results in a 6% increase in relative efficacy in non-IC, while increasing the titer from 1:500 to 1:1000 only yields a 3% increase (Table 2 ). A similar trend is observed in the updated data from Schmidt et al., where efficacy shows diminishing effect above titers of approximately 1:500, suggesting that further increases may offer limited additional benefit [ 6 ]. Therefore, both methods indicate that very high titers, which would require significantly higher mAb doses and more burdensome route of administration (i.e., IV), may not be preferable from a risk–benefit and healthcare utilization perspective.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: The lower efficacy observed in cohort A (IC) in comparison to the cohort B (non-IC) (Fig. 2 ) align with results from the prevalence-adjusted titer-based threshold of protection (ToP) model developed using data from the PROVENT (evaluation of tixagevimab/cilgavimab in non-IC participants) and SUPERNOVA (evaluation of sipavibart in IC participants) studies [ 15 ]. In that model, a titer of approximately 1:200 corresponds to 40% efficacy when IC and non-IC data were analyzed together; analysis of IC only was not performed. The CANOPY cohort A model predicts a titer of 1:200 associated with 45% efficacy for IC population (Table 2 ), which is similar to the ToP model. Models developed using data from one platform (e.g., AstraZeneca platform) may have limitations in applicability to other mAbs (e.g., Invivyd platform) given differences in Fc modifications, innate effector functions, specific epitopes targeted, and other biophysical properties inherent to mAbs. Nevertheless, data such as these provide useful information to further support the novel data analytics employed with the CANOPY dataset to ascertain a reasonable titer correlate of protection.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Based on the Cox model, a titer target of approximately 1:500 for dominant circulating variants with the highest IC 50 would ensure coverage against the spectrum of relevant circulating variants representing a range of IC 50 values, as well as confer clinically meaningful protection in both IC and non-IC patients. Moreover, this target also safeguards against variant evolution, where a reduction in titer by approximately half (i.e., 1:250) equates to less than a 5% decrease in efficacy for the IC and non-IC populations. As higher titer targets (e.g., > 1:500) necessitate higher overall administered dose with limited efficacy gain based on the Cox model, a titer target of 1:500 is reasonable and supported from both an efficacy and risk–benefit perspective in both IC and non-IC populations.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Following selection of a titer target, immunobridging calculations can be employed. For example, CANOPY utilized the lower limit of the 90% CI of the GMR between the selected titer target and the mAb/variant-specific neutralizing titer > 0.8.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: While the confidence bands (Fig. 2 ) are wide, they appropriately reflect the true uncertainty in the data rather than the deficiency in the model. The variability arises from assay-level measurement error, sample size, and limited infection events at high concentrations (neutralization levels). The bootstrap procedure is conservative and correctly propagates this uncertainty through the Cox model. Narrower intervals would require stronger assumptions or additional data (e.g., larger sample size or repeated measurements), rather than alternative modeling choices.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Importantly, as the Cox model is derived from the pemivibart efficacy data through 12 months and is variant-agnostic based on implementation of a weighted IC 50 , it can serve as a predictive tool for establishing platform-relevant (i.e., mAbs engineered with same structural backbone, Fc region modifications, and associated effector functions) titer targets and corresponding doses with which to immunobridge any future Invivyd platform mAbs with a high degree of certainty of translated clinical benefit. As the antiviral activity of pemivibart is routinely evaluated to assess activity against contemporary circulating variants, this approach is generalizable [ 7 ].

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: Interpretation of this work should be viewed in light of several limitations. First, placebo data from cohort B were used as the control group for cohort A in the model. However, the fact that both CANOPY cohorts were conducted concurrently helps mitigate this limitation by providing a temporally aligned reference from the placebo arm in cohort B. In addition, the partially contemporaneous placebo-controlled SUPERNOVA trial, which evaluated sipavibart in an IC population during the JN.1 surge, reported a similar 6-month attack rate in its comparator arm (12% in CANOPY vs 11% in SUPERNOVA) [ 12 ], further supporting the external validity of the CANOPY cohort B placebo data as a reference for modeling efficacy in the IC population. Second, the model does not control for baseline immunity and further information is provided in the Supplementary Material. Third, the use of a variant-weighted IC 50 approach and log-linear titer-efficacy relationship assumption may partially attenuate sudden changes in neutralization sensitivity associated with rapid emergence of resistant variants. However, this approach avoids sharp fluctuations in model estimates driven by short-lived or uncertain changes in variant sensitivity while preserving a stable, interpretable, and statistically reliable association between higher sVNA levels and lower infection risk.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: The data from CANOPY provide a robust foundation, incorporating both neutralizing titers and long-term clinical efficacy, to support evaluation of the target titers used to guide dosing, predict estimated clinical benefit for related mAbs developed on the same platform, and support immunobridging. This surrogate approach can be effectively derived using the proposed Cox model.

metadata.title: A Statistical Immune Correlates of Protection Model for Predicting Efficacy from Neutralizing Antibody Titers to Establish Immunobridging of Monoclonal Antibodies for Prevention of COVID-19.

text: COVID-19 is still a global emergency with no effective treatment.[ 1 ] Vaccination is currently the best option for disaster management.[ 2 ] COVID-19 vaccination is now universally acknowledged as an effective primary COVID-19–preventive strategy. COVID-19 vaccination has been shown to be beneficial in preventing the disease. In most cases, two vaccine doses are given. It is necessary to administer two intramuscular vaccine doses. Many scientists provide strategies for reducing the risk and overcoming the problem of insufficient COVID-19 vaccination supplies by administering COVID-19 in a different way. Intradermal administration was proposed as a viable method for delivering reciprocal dosages while lowering vaccine costs and quantities.[ 3 ] In fact, when compared to full-dose immunization, fractional intradermal vaccination has been shown to be more immunogenic and effective. Indeed, for various pathogens, including influenza virus, rabies virus, poliovirus, hepatitis B virus, and hepatitis A virus, the immunogenicity and efficacy of fractional intradermal vaccination in comparison to full-dose immunization have been investigated and verified.[ 3 ] The cost and effectiveness of alternative vaccination administration are also crucial factors to consider. In some cases, the intradermal COVID-19 version is already in use.

metadata.title: Cost-effectiveness analysis of intradermal versus classical intramuscular COVID-19 vaccine administration: Specific concern on update situation due to emerging Omicron variant.

text: Concerns about the efficacy of the COVID-19 vaccination are currently focused on the impact of a new severe acute respiratory syndrome–related coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) variant. The Omicron variant is a current issue. Africa was the first place where this variation was discovered. The Omicron variation has ravaged numerous populations just 2 months after it originated, and it is quickly becoming the dominant coronavirus strain. Although global case numbers have reached new highs, the proportion of hospitalizations and deaths is lower than in prior outbreaks. The mutation inside the Omicron variation has been discovered and linked to decreased vaccination effectiveness. The decrease in vaccine efficacy has become a major source of concern. In comparison to prior variants of concern, Omicron has a considerable number of changes in S, most of which are centered around the receptor binding motif: 30 amino acid substitutions, six residue deletions, and three residue insertions.[ 1 ] Other regions (receptor binding domain and N-terminal domain) have mutations that could influence neutralizing antibodies. It is feared that Omicron will increase the likelihood of infecting those who have received vaccines with antigens based on the original S sequence.

metadata.title: Cost-effectiveness analysis of intradermal versus classical intramuscular COVID-19 vaccine administration: Specific concern on update situation due to emerging Omicron variant.

text: However, when the vaccine is delivered in a different way, the efficacy and cost-effectiveness of the vaccination become highly interesting topics. The authors compare the cost-effectiveness of alternative intradermal COVID-19 vaccine delivery to regular intramuscular COVID-19 vaccine administration in this study, with specific reference to the present problematic Omicron strain of SARS-CoV-2. This study focuses solely on mRNA COVID-19 vaccine delivery. The standard two-dose vaccine has been conditioned to a large extent. The authors examined two vaccine administration routes: intradermal and intramuscular injections. According to a recent paper, only 20% of the standard intramuscular mRNA vaccine dosage is injected intradermally for intradermal vaccine administration.[ 3 ] The unit cost of vaccine administration in our case (referencing local data, https://covid-19.kapook.com/view241253.html ) is used in this work. According to publicly available information, one dose of COVID-19 administered through intramuscular injection costs 34.32 euros. To address the effect of the Omicron variant, the new data based on the study in our study are referred to. According to that study, the new developing variation causes a 20.8-fold drop in vaccine efficacy.[ 4 ] A direct total cost comparison of intradermal versus conventional intramuscular COVID-19 vaccination was undertaken for cost comparison.

metadata.title: Cost-effectiveness analysis of intradermal versus classical intramuscular COVID-19 vaccine administration: Specific concern on update situation due to emerging Omicron variant.

text: The investigation’s major data source is the publicly available information, which has already been described. For the purpose of analysis, the reported intended vaccination price is assigned to cost, while the indicated vaccine efficacy is assigned to effectiveness. Each COVID-19 vaccine administration’s cost per effectiveness is determined, and the cost-effectiveness value of vaccine administration is compared. According to a cost-effectiveness study with a specific focus on preventing the Omicron variant of SARS-CoV-2, the cost per unit efficacy of using intramuscular COVID-19 vaccine injection is higher than that of intradermal immunization [ Table 1 ].

metadata.title: Cost-effectiveness analysis of intradermal versus classical intramuscular COVID-19 vaccine administration: Specific concern on update situation due to emerging Omicron variant.

text: The researchers utilized a cost-effectiveness analysis to look at the cost and efficacy of the new COVID-19 vaccines, which will be available to the general public soon. The cost is calculated directly from public data, as previously indicated. In terms of efficacy, the previous comparison study’s reported that vaccine efficacy is mentioned. The efficiency of COVID-19 vaccination is measured as the rate of SARS-CoV-2 antireceptor binding domain antibody response relative to no immunization 1 month later. According to the referring study, the efficiency of intradermal versus normal intramuscular COVID-19 vaccine administration against nonvariant strains is equal to 38 and 75 times, respectively.[ 3 ] An adjustment is made via direct mathematical extraction of reduced vaccine efficacy due to the Omicron form as previously discussed.[ 4 ] According to the model, the final derived effectiveness will be equal to the reported efficacy of the naive strain minus the reported reduced efficacy of the vaccine.

metadata.title: Cost-effectiveness analysis of intradermal versus classical intramuscular COVID-19 vaccine administration: Specific concern on update situation due to emerging Omicron variant.

text: In the case of the nonvariant strain, a recent investigation suggested that intradermal COVID-19 vaccine injections elicited a strong immunological response. Despite a change in vaccine efficacy, when the cost-effectiveness model is applied to the Omicron form, intradermal COVID-19 vaccine injection has a 1.59-fold lower cost per effectiveness in necessary cost per unit effectiveness. For preventing the Omicron form, intradermal vaccination is a more cost-effective and practical option than the traditional intramuscular COVID-19 vaccine. Intradermal COVID-19 vaccine is still more cost-effective than standard intramuscular COVID-19 vaccination, according to the current studies.

metadata.title: Cost-effectiveness analysis of intradermal versus classical intramuscular COVID-19 vaccine administration: Specific concern on update situation due to emerging Omicron variant.

text: The COVID-19 pandemic represents a severe global public health challenge. Vaccination is a cornerstone of control, yet the efficacy of primary regimens wanes over time, exacerbated by immune-evasive variants like Omicron ( Andrews et al., 2022 ; Collie et al., 2022 ; Nordström et al., 2022 ). While previous study has showed that heterologous prime-boost strategies with different COVID-19 vaccines enhanced neutralizing antibodies and broaden cross-reactivity ( Jin, Guo, et al., 2023 ; Jin, Tang, et al., 2023 ; Li, Hou, et al., 2022 ; Li, Wu et al., 2022 ) . Although an increasing immune response associated with heterologous prime-boost regimen has been demonstrated, their protective superiority is inherently hampered by the rapid evolution and immune escape of SARS-CoV-2 variants. Conventional clinical trials often lag behind viral evolution, failing to provide timely efficacy data against circulating strains ( Carabelli et al., 2023 ). In addition, efficacy trials of vaccines are costly, time-consuming, and ethical restrictions ( Black, 2015 ). In the context of rapidly evolving viruses and emerging variants, relying solely on clinical trial study is insufficient for timely response to a changing pandemic landscape.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: This critical lag necessitates alternative approaches. Neutralizing antibodies, strongly correlated with protection against infection across vaccine platforms and endorsed as correlates of protection (CoPs) by regulatory agencies, offer a key immunological metric ( Earle et al., 2021 ; Gilbert et al., 2022 ; Regev-Yochay et al., 2023 ). Evidence from natural infection cohorts has demonstrated a strong positive correlation between neutralizing antibody titers and protection against reinfection ( Addetia et al., 2020 ), with high antibody levels effectively preventing reinfection by circulating variants of concern ( Garcia-Beltran et al., 2021 ). Consequently, neutralizing antibodies have been endorsed by both the U.S. Food and Drug Administration and the European Medicines Agency as immunological correlates of protection in support of emergency use authorization for COVID-19 vaccines ( EMA, 2021 ; FDA, 2022 ). Khoury et al. integrated neutralizing antibody data from COVID-19 vaccines to reveal a nonlinear relationship between neutralizing antibodies and vaccine efficacy. The model relies on neutralizing antibody levels against specific strains post-vaccination. When new variants dominated, neutralizing antibody data for the new strain was required to predict efficacy ( Khoury et al., 2021 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Genetic distance quantifies the sequence divergence between viral variants, reflecting key antigenic site mutations and immune escape potential, and serves as a critical metric for evaluating vaccine-induced immunity ( Cerqueira-Silva, de Araujo Oliveira, et al., 2022 ; Cerqueira-Silva, de Araujo Oliveira, et al., 2022 ; Ella et al., 2021 ; Suphanchaimat et al., 2022 ). Therefore, the “genetic distance between viral variants and quantifying antigenic site divergence and immune escape potential” is a principal determinant of vaccine effectiveness, linking viral evolution to immune protection.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: In this study, we developed a predictive model linking neutralizing antibody levels, genetic distance, and vaccine protection in order to address the challenge of timely efficacy assessment amidst evolving variants. Utilizing neutralization data from previous heterologous booster trials and introducing a refined genetic distance calculation focused on key RBD residues, our model enables rapid evaluation of heterologous booster efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Neutralizing antibody data from three previously reported heterologous prime-boost clinical trials of COVID-19 vaccines ( ClinicalTrials.gov registration numbers: NCT04892459 ( Li, Wu, et al., 2022 ), NCT04952727 ( Jin, Tang, et al., 2023 ), and NCT05043259 ( Li, Wu, et al., 2022 ); Supplementary Appendix 1 ) were used to construct the exponential decay models of antibody kinetics, which were subsequently applied to vaccine efficacy prediction. The three trial protocols were approved by Research Ethics Committee of Jiangsu Provincial Center for Disease Control and Prevention (JSJK2021-A007-01, JSJK2021-A008-01 and JSJK2021-A024-02), and were conducted following the principles of the Declaration of Helsinki, ICH Good Clinical Practice guidelines and local guidelines. Written informed consent was obtained from all participants. We established a “neutralizing antibody-vaccine efficacy” (NAb-VE) model based on two components: (1) published vaccine efficacy or effectiveness data from COVID-19 priming regimens and heterologous prime-boost regimens; and (2) published live-virus neutralization titres from the corresponding regimens. This model enabled prediction of both the magnitude and durability of protection conferred by different prime-boost vaccination strategies against SARS-CoV-2 variants (i.e. wild-type SARS-CoV-2, Delta, Omicron BA.1) for which neutralizing antibody titres were available.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Then, in order to extend the model predictions to other variants for which neutralizing antibody titres were unavailable, we established a second efficacy prediction model—the “modified genetic distance-vaccine efficacy” (GD-VE) model based on a combination of three components: (1) published vaccine efficacy or effectiveness data from heterologous COVID-19 prime-boost regimens; (2) predicted efficacy estimates derived from the NAb-VE model; and (3) viral genomic sequence data from the Global Initiative on Sharing All Influenza Data (GISAID) database.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: At last, we developed a sieve analysis framework-the “neutralizing antibody-genetic distance-vaccine efficacy” (NAb-GD-VE) model to predict average vaccine efficacy against symptomatic COVID-19 diseases by integrating the NAb-VE and GD-VE models. Using monthly whole-genome sequencing data during the pandemic and post-pandemic periods (July 2021-July 2023) in Jiangsu Province, China, we estimated temporal changes in efficacy across different vaccination strategies as SARS-CoV-2 variants circulated. This prediction assumed that, for months without reported COVID-19 cases, the genetic distance was taken as identical to that of the preceding month with circulating variants to ensure continuity in monthly estimates. The study evaluated three vaccination regimens: (1) two doses of inactivated CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV booster; (2) two doses of CoronaVac followed by an intramuscular Ad5-nCoV booster; and (3) three doses of homologous CoronaVac.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: In order to establish NAb-VE model and GD-VE model, we systematically searched PubMed and Web of Science for studies on COVID-19 vaccine immunogenicity and efficacy or effectiveness ( Supplementary Appendix 2 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: A total of 23 studies were included to establish the models ( Fig. S3 ). The NAb-VE model was fitted using data from 9 studies reporting live-virus neutralizing antibody titres standardized to convalescent sera (available either directly or indirectly) induced by COVID-19 vaccines ( Angkasekwinai et al., 2024 ; Folegatti et al., 2020 ; Jackson et al., 2020 ; Keech et al., 2020 ; Logunov et al., 2020 ; Sadoff et al., 2021 ; Walsh et al., 2020 ; Wheatley et al., 2021 ; Zhang et al., 2021 ), as well as 9 studies reporting vaccine efficacy or effectiveness against symptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection ( Baden et al., 2021 ; Jara et al., 2022 ; Logunov et al., 2021 ; Lumley et al., 2021 ; Novavax, 2021 ; Pearson, Magalhaes, & Deng, 2021 ; Polack et al., 2020 ; Sacks, 2021 ; Voysey et al., 2021 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: For the GD-VE model, to enable subsequent application in predicting the efficacy of heterologous boosters after inactivated vaccine priming, we combined two data sources: (i) efficacy estimates derived from the NAb-VE model for three regimens—two doses of CoronaVac followed by aerosolized Ad5-nCoV booster, two doses of CoronaVac followed by intramuscular Ad5-nCoV booster, and three doses of homologous CoronaVac; and (ii) observed effectiveness data from five studies of heterologous prime-boost regimens: two doses of CoronaVac followed by BNT162b2 (reported in all five studies ( Cerqueira-Silva, de Araujo Oliveira, et al., 2022 ; Cerqueira-Silva, de Araujo Oliveira, et al., 2022 ; Jara et al., 2022 ; Sritipsukho et al., 2022 ; Suphanchaimat et al., 2022 )) and two doses of CoronaVac followed by ChAdOx1 (reported in three studies ( Jara et al., 2022 ; Sritipsukho et al., 2022 ; Suphanchaimat et al., 2022 )). A total of 87,036 full-length SARS-CoV-2 genome sequences (FASTA format) corresponding to the time and location of the five studies were downloaded from the GISAID EpiCoV database ( https://gisaid.org/ ) and were used to compute genetic distances for the GD-VE model construction ( Supplementary Appendix 3 ). In addition, 1701 full-length genome sequences were collected from Jiangsu Province, China, between July 2021 and July 2023, were downloaded from the GISAID EpiCoV database. These sequences were used to calculate genetic distances and to apply the NAb-GD-VE sieve model for predicting the dynamic vaccine efficacy during periods of overlapping variant circulation.

text: Following the Prentice Criteria outlined in the WHO report on evaluating immunological surrogate endpoints for vaccine protection ( Supplementary Appendix 4 )( WHO, 2013 ), we assessed whether neutralizing antibody titres against live SARS-CoV-2 virus could serve as an immunological surrogate endpoint for protection conferred by COVID-19 vaccination regimens.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The model used a logistic relationship to link neutralizing antibody levels to protection probability and applied maximum likelihood estimation to fit the model and determine optimal parameters for population-level vaccine efficacy. This model was based on the fundamental assumptions: (i) the vaccine efficacy was primarily mediated by neutralizing antibodies; (ii) live-virus neutralizing antibodies declined exponentially over time; and (iii) their levels were linked to efficacy through a logistic function ( Supplementary Appendix 5 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The model was internally validated using leave-one-out cross-validation and externally validated with observed vaccine efficacy data from heterologous prime-boost regimens not included in the model. Sensitivity analysis was conducted using the 95% CI bounds of the mean neutralizing antibody levels to predict efficacy ( Supplementary Appendix 5.4 ). Agreement between model-predicted and observed efficacy values was assessed using the Concordance Correlation Coefficient (CCC). This modeling framework was originally proposed by Khoury et al. ( Khoury et al., 2021 ), and we further refined the model by incorporating heterologous prime-boost vaccination data.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: We utilized live virus neutralizing antibody data from three previously conducted clinical trials on heterologous prime-boost COVID-19 vaccination regimens ( ClinicalTrials.gov registration numbers: NCT04892459 ( Li, Hou, et al., 2022 ), NCT04952727 ( Jin, Guo, et al., 2023 ), and NCT05043259 ( Li, Wu, et al., 2022 )) to fit an exponential decay model ( Supplementary Appendix 5.5 ). This was then incorporated into the NAb-VE model to estimate the durability of vaccine-induced protection.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The genetic distance-based prediction model was constructed using a two-level linear mixed-effects model ( Cao et al., 2022 ). This model relies on three assumptions: (i) immune escape of SARS-CoV-2 variants after heterologous prime-boost regimens was primarily determined by genetic distance; (ii) vaccine efficacy declines linearly with increasing genetic distance; (iii) and covariates such as age and time since booster acted independently of genetic distance ( Supplementary Appendix 6 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Previous studies have commonly used the Hamming distance to calculate genetic distance between viral sequences, assigning a uniform weight of 1.0 to each amino acid mismatch and computing the average number of mismatches ( Cao et al., 2022 ). In the present study, we accounted for the fact that key mutations tend to accumulate at antigenic sites within the receptor-binding domain (RBD) of SARS-CoV-2 variants—mutations that substantially alter antigenicity and facilitate escape from neutralizing antibodies (NAbs) elicited by repeated vaccination or infection. A total of 13 critical amino acid positions were selected as follows: R346, K378, K417, K444, V445, G446, N450, L452, V483, E484, F486, F490, and Q493 ( Jian et al., 2025 ). These 13 sites repeatedly mutate across multiple SARS-CoV-2 variant lineages, enhancing viral infectivity and increasing escape from monoclonal antibody capture ( Jian et al., 2025 ). To reflect the increased immunological relevance of these sites, we assigned greater weights to mismatches occurring at these critical antigenic positions when calculating the average number of amino acid mismatches, thereby defining a modified genetic distance. Based on the deep mutational scanning (DMS) scores of these key mutations reflecting immune escape in vitro, which differed by up to twofold, we experimentally set the weight coefficients for key antigenic sites to 1.0, 1.5, and 2.0, as no related model parameters have been reported previously ( Dadonaite et al., 2023 ; Jian et al., 2025 ). Using these weights, we recalculated the modified genetic distances and subsequently fitted each to the GD-VE model ( Supplementary Appendix 6.2 ). The performance of each model was evaluated by calculating the CCC between the predicted and validated vaccine efficacy values. The weight yielding the highest CCC was selected as optimal.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The GD-VE model was validated similarly to the NAb-VE model, using leave-one-out cross-validation, external data, sensitivity analysis, and agreement assessed with the CCC ( Supplementary Appendix 6.4 ).

text: We developed a composite sieve analysis framework of the NAb-GD-VE model using a weighted averaging approach under conditions of overlapping and sequential circulation of diverse SARS-CoV-2 variants ( Supplementary Appendix 7 ). As this model represents an integration of the validated NAb-VE and GD-VE models, no separate validation was performed.

text: Molecular phylogenetic analysis of full-length SARS-CoV-2 genomes was performed to explore the relationship between predicted vaccine efficacy and genetic divergence of variants ( Supplementary Appendix 8 ).

text: All statistical analyses and data visualizations were performed using R software (version 4.2.3). When the predicted efficacy boundary exceeds the predefined bounds of 0.00% and 100.00%, a truncation method has been applied to ensure statistical validity. The primary R packages used were listed in Table S4 . The cloud-based online prediction system was developed using Python (version 3.0) with the Flask framework (see Supplementary Appendix 10 for system development details).

text: The results showed that neutralizing antibodies can thus serve as valid immunological surrogate endpoints for COVID-19 vaccine protection satisfying the Prentice criteria ( Supplementary Appendix 11 ). In this study, the fitted parameter values for the NAb-VE model were σ = 0.4550, k = 2.2924 (95% CI: 1.8612-2.8234), and n 50 = 0.1784 (95% CI: 0.1192-0.2669) ( Fig. 1 a).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: As the fold decrease in neutralizing antibodies against wild-type SARS-CoV-2 increases, predicted efficacy against symptomatic infection declined for all three regimens: two-dose inactivated CoronaVac plus an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV, two-dose CoronaVac plus one-dose intramuscular Ad5-nCoV, and three-dose CoronaVac, with a more gradual decline for those showing higher protection against the wild-type strain ( Fig. 1 b).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The internal and external validation results showed close agreement between predicted and observed efficacy ( Fig. 1 c and d). The error was almost always within 5% ( Table S8 and Table S9 ). The calibration plot showed strong concordance between predicted and observed efficacy, with a CCC of 0.95 (95% CI: 0.82-0.98) ( Fig. 1 e). Sensitivity analysis confirmed the robustness of the model predictions ( Fig. 1 f and Table S10 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The GD-VE model results showed that genetic distances in various SARS-CoV-2 genomic regions, including RBD, were significantly linked to protection efficacy against symptomatic COVID-19 (P < 0.05). RBD genetic distance, influencing viral binding to ACE2, was selected, with each RBD mutation associated with a 2.27% reduction in protection ( Table S11 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: To capture the immunological significance of RBD antigenic sites, we applied weighted schemes to key positions and recalculated genetic distances for GD-VE model fitting. A weight coefficient of 2.0 yielded the highest CCC in validation and was adopted as the optimal weighting strategy ( Table S12 ). With the wild-type SARS-CoV-2 strain as the reference, modified RBD genetic distances for variants ranged from 3 for Delta to 37 for XDV.1 ( Table S13 ).

text: The GD-VE model achieved an R 2 of 85.53% ( Table S15 ) with intercept and error term standard deviations of 28.26 and 13.33, respectively ( Table S17 ). The model's predicted relationship was shown in Fig. 2 a. Regression diagnostics indicated a good fit of the GD-VE model ( Figure S6- S8 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Internal and external validation showed that across all heterologous prime-boost regimens, predicted and observed efficacy differed by <10%, with overlapping 95% CIs ( Fig. 2 b and Table S20 ; Fig. 2 c and Table S23 ). The CCC between the predicted and observed efficacy values in internal and external validation was 0.93 (95% CI: 0.49-0.99), indicating strong agreement between predicted and actual outcomes ( Fig. 2 d). Sensitivity analyses further confirmed the robustness of the GD-VE model ( Fig. 2 e and Table S24 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The validated NAb-VE and GD-VE models predicted that, among the three heterologous prime-boost regimens, two doses of CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV booster conferred the highest predictive efficacy against symptomatic COVID-19, followed by the intramuscular Ad5-nCoV booster, whereas the homologous three-dose CoronaVac regimen provided the lowest predictive protection. For the regimen of two doses of CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV, predicted efficacy was 95.40% (95% CI: 92.67%-98.13%) against the wild-type strain, 79.56% (95% CI: 53.31%-100.00%) against Delta, and 68.21% (95% CI: 42.53%-93.89%) against Omicron BA.5.2.20. Against other Omicron sublineages (BA.2.75, BA.1, BF.5 and BF.7), predicted efficacy fell below 50%, and for EG.1.2, XBB.1.16, EG.5.1, JN.1, XDV.1, KP.2, and KP.3, the model predicted little to no protection.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: Predicted efficacy was 87.32% (95% CI: 83.86-90.79) for two doses of CoronaVac followed by an intramuscular Ad5-nCoV booster and 61.40% (56.64-66.17) for three doses of CoronaVac against the wild-type strain. However, for nearly all other variants, both regimens exhibited <50% predicted efficacy or no measurable protection estimates ( Fig. 3 a and Table S25 ).

text: A phylogenetic tree based on whole-genome sequences of 14 SARS-CoV-2 strains and 29,743 nucleotide positions showed that closer evolutionary relationships corresponded to more similar predicted protective efficacy against symptomatic infection under heterologous prime-boost regimens ( Fig. S9 ).

text: Between July 2021 and July 2023 in Jiangsu, China, multiple SARS-CoV-2 variants cross-circulated. In this context, sieve model analyses showed that two doses of CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV booster consistently provided the highest predicted efficacy against symptomatic COVID-19. However, after Omicron sublineages emerged, the predicted efficacy of all three regimens dropped below 50% ( Fig. 4 ).

text: Neutralizing antibody data obtained from clinical trials were used to fit exponential decay models for each SARS-CoV-2 strain, with estimated half-lives summarized in Table S26-S28 .

text: The GD-VE model predicted that two doses of CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV booster provided the most durable estimated protection against symptomatic infection with the wild-type strain, with efficacy of 76.98% (95% CI: 70.32%-83.63%) at 12 months and 36.76% (95% CI: 31.71%-41.81%) at 24 months post-boost. Efficacy at 12 months for the two doses of CoronaVac followed by an intramuscular Ad5-nCoV and three-dose CoronaVac regimens was 24.39% (95% CI: 17.85%-30.94%) and 9.33% (95% CI: 3.34%-15.33%), respectively, both declining to nearly zero by 24 months post-boost ( Fig. 3 c and Table S29 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: For the Delta variant, two doses of CoronaVac followed by an intramuscular Ad5-nCoV regimen yielded predicted efficacies below 50% throughout, while the three-dose CoronaVac regimen remained consistently below 15% ( Fig. 3 d and Table S30 ). Durability efficacy estimates for the two doses of CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized Ad5-nCoV regimen were not analyzed for Delta, as neutralizing antibody measurements for this variant were not pre-specified in the protocol.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: For the Omicron BA.1 variant, both the two doses of CoronaVac followed by an aerosolized or intramuscular Ad5-nCoV regimens conferred relatively low levels of predicted protection, while the homologous three-dose CoronaVac regimen provided virtually none ( Fig. 3 e and Table S31 ).

text: We further assumed that the half-life of neutralizing antibodies against variants with different genetic distances was the same as that against the wild-type strain, enabling prediction of vaccine efficacy over time and genetic distance. Predicted efficacy declined with both time and increasing genetic distance across all three regimens ( Fig. 3 b).

text: Additionally, we developed a cloud-based online prediction platform for the NAb-VE model, accessible at: http://8.153.70.192:1003/ . The platform requires no login, and international users can directly perform efficacy predictions. The system functionality and interface were shown in Fig. S10 . We demonstrated the utility of the platform using two case examples, which confirmed its reliable predictive performance ( Supplementary Appendix 20 ).

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: The ongoing evolution of SARS-CoV-2 and the risk of future respiratory pandemics highlight the need for efficient tools to evaluate vaccine-induced protection ( Bardhan et al., 2024 ; WHO, 2024 ). This study established a predictive framework that integrates neutralizing antibody titers and viral genetic distance to estimate the efficacy and durability of heterologous prime-boost immunization. This model enables real-time estimation of protection against symptomatic infection caused by specific SARS-CoV-2 variants, quantifies the durability of efficacy across heterologous prime-boost regimens, and evaluates average efficacy under overlapping variant circulation. The framework provides a scalable approach to inform vaccine deployment in future outbreaks.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: In developing the neutralizing antibody-based protection model, we built upon the framework established by Khoury et al. ( Khoury et al., 2021 ), optimizing model parameters using data from heterologous prime-boost immunization studies and adjusting for covariates such as pre-existing Ad5 immunity, which may impair the generation of protective antibodies induced by Ad5-nCoV( Li et al., 2023 ; Tang et al., 2023 ). Our optimized model better reflects the characteristics of heterologous boosting with adenoviral vector following inactivated vaccine priming.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: In this study, we developed a genetic distance-based protection prediction model to estimate the efficacy of different heterologous prime-boost regimens against emerging variants with unknown neutralizing antibody levels, thereby addressing a limitation of the neutralization-based models. We found that amino acid substitutions in the RBD significantly affected the predicted efficacy of heterologous regimen against symptomatic SARS-CoV-2 infection—consistent with previous findings in the context of primary vaccination ( Cao et al., 2022 ). The RBD is the principal target of neutralizing antibodies, and most COVID-19 vaccines (e.g., mRNA and inactivated vaccines) confer protection primarily by inducing RBD-specific neutralizing antibodies ( Liang et al., 2025 ; Liu et al., 2023 ). Mutations in the RBD can alter antibody-binding sites, reducing the binding affinity of neutralizing antibodies and thereby diminishing vaccine-induced protection ( Ao et al., 2022 ). SARS-CoV-2 variants continue to accumulate recurrent mutations at key antigenic sites within the RBD, leading to increased infectivity and enhanced escape from both monoclonal antibodies and vaccine or infection-induced neutralizing antibodies ( Jian et al., 2023 , 2025 ; Kaku et al., 2023 ; Yang et al., 2024 ). Our framework is the first to define a modified RBD genetic distance that assigns greater weight to key antigenic sites, enhancing the metric's ability to capture biologically meaningful variation relevant to immune escape and vaccine performance.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: This study establishes a cloud-based prediction system for rapid estimation of vaccine efficacy (with 95% CIs) against specific SARS-CoV-2 variants via an intuitive web interface. The platform delivers real-time visualization, stable performance, and requires no statistical expertise. Its generalizable framework allows adaptation to other respiratory pathogens following parameter validation, serving as a scalable solution for vaccine evaluation and pandemic preparedness. By leveraging existing viral surveillance infrastructure, this system enables real-time efficacy assessment during multi-variant circulation and provides a technical foundation for evaluating vaccines against future emerging respiratory threats.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: This study has several limitations. Firstly, WHO reference standards may cause slight underestimation of predicted efficacy, though this effect attenuates at higher antibody levels. Secondly, the neutralization models used clinical trial efficacy data while genetic distance models incorporated real-world effectiveness data. Thirdly, this study is limited by its focus on heterologous sequential booster regimens following inactivated COVID-19 vaccines, primarily due to data availability. Expanding the external validation dataset to include additional vaccine types and diverse population characteristics would be essential for improving the model's generalizability and prediction accuracy in various scenarios. This limitation should be addressed in future research. Finally, a significant limitation of our model is the exclusive focus on humoral immunity, which does not account for the roles of cellular immunity (e.g., T-cell responses) and mucosal immunity. Both cellular and mucosal immunity play crucial roles in the protection against SARS-CoV-2 infection, particularly in preventing severe disease and influencing viral clearance. Excluding these immune responses could lead to an incomplete understanding of vaccine efficacy, especially against variants with immune escape capabilities. Future models should integrate data on T-cell responses, mucosal immunity, and their interactions with neutralizing antibodies to provide a more comprehensive evaluation of vaccine-induced protection. This would help reduce potential biases and enhance the accuracy and applicability of the prediction.

metadata.title: A predictive model for rapid assessment of protective efficacy against emerging SARS-CoV-2 variants.

text: This study developed a framework for evaluating the efficacy of heterologous prime-boost COVID-19 vaccination based on neutralizing antibodies and viral genetic distance. By leveraging existing viral surveillance networks, the framework enables real-time estimation of vaccine efficacy under co-circulating variant scenarios and offers a technical foundation for timely evaluation of vaccine efficacy against future emerging respiratory pathogens.
